_meta.note: POC corpus (neurology). DRAFT patient-education content grounded in PUBLIC-DOMAIN sources (NINDS, MedlinePlus, CDC) plus general clinical patient-education knowledge. NOT clinically reviewed — every canonical_answer MUST be reviewed and owned by a clinician (set reviewed_by + last_reviewed) before ANY non-internal/patient exposure. Answers are deliberately general and defer on anything individualized (specific doses, personal timelines). Emergency thresholds (seizure 911 criteria, BE-FAST) grounded against CDC/MedlinePlus 2026-06-29. See faq_sources_bibliography.md.
_meta.escalation_legend: none = answer plainly | soft = answer + defer-to-prescriber/team | route = handled by guardrails (not stored here)

entries.question: What should I expect in the first few weeks of stroke recovery?
entries.canonical_answer: Recovery is different for everyone. The early weeks usually focus on rehabilitation, which may include physical, occupational, and speech therapy, and on gradually rebuilding strength and daily function. Fatigue and emotional ups and downs are common in this period.

entries.question: When can I drive after a stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: Driving after a stroke depends on how it affected your vision, movement, thinking, and reaction time, and many places require a waiting period and a clinician's clearance. Most people are asked not to drive until their care team has evaluated them.

entries.question: How can I lower my risk of another stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: In general, lowering stroke risk focuses on managing blood pressure, taking prescribed medications consistently, not smoking, staying active, and eating a heart-healthy diet. Your care team will tailor a prevention plan to you, so it's worth asking which steps matter most in your case.

entries.question: Is it normal to feel very tired after a stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: Yes, fatigue is very common after a stroke and can last weeks to months as your brain and body recover. Pacing activities and rest often help. If tiredness is sudden, severe, or comes with new symptoms, contact your care team.

entries.question: Is it normal to feel sad or emotional after a stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: Mood changes, including sadness, anxiety, frustration, and tearfulness, are common after a stroke and can have both emotional and physical causes. This is treatable, so please tell your care team how you're feeling, especially if it's persistent.

entries.question: When is my follow-up appointment after a stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: Follow-up timing varies, but many people are seen within a few weeks of leaving the hospital, sometimes with neurology, primary care, and therapy visits. Your discharge paperwork should list your specific appointments.

entries.question: What stroke warning signs should make me call 911?
entries.canonical_answer: Use BE-FAST: sudden Balance loss, Eye or vision changes, Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech trouble. The T is Time — call 911 right away if any of these appear suddenly, even if they go away. Other warning signs include sudden numbness, confusion, or a sudden severe headache.

entries.question: What is a TIA or mini-stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: A TIA, sometimes called a mini-stroke, is a brief episode of stroke-like symptoms that resolves on its own. Even though it goes away, it is a serious warning sign and needs urgent medical evaluation, because it can signal a higher risk of a full stroke.

entries.question: Can I exercise after a stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: Most people are encouraged to gradually become more active after a stroke, often starting with a therapy or rehab program. The right type and pace of exercise depends on your recovery, so check with your care team before starting.

entries.question: When can I go back to work after a stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: Returning to work depends on your recovery, your job's demands, and how the stroke affected you. Some people return within weeks, others need longer or a gradual return. Your care team and any therapists can help guide the timing.

entries.question: What are my stroke medications generally for?
entries.canonical_answer: After a stroke, people are often prescribed medicines that fall into a few general groups: blood thinners or antiplatelets to reduce clot risk, blood-pressure medicines, and cholesterol-lowering medicines like statins. Taking them as prescribed is an important part of preventing another stroke; ask your prescriber what each of yours does.

entries.question: Is there a recommended diet after a stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: In general, a heart-healthy eating pattern is recommended after a stroke: plenty of vegetables and fruit, whole grains, and less salt, saturated fat, and processed food. If you have swallowing problems or other conditions, your care team or a dietitian can tailor advice for you.

entries.question: What do my follow-up blood tests check?
entries.canonical_answer: Follow-up bloodwork in neurology often checks things like medication levels, kidney and liver function, blood counts, or cholesterol and blood sugar, depending on your condition and medicines. Your care team orders them based on what they're monitoring for you.

entries.question: Why do I need a follow-up MRI or CT scan?
entries.canonical_answer: Repeat imaging is often used to see how something is healing or changing over time, to compare against an earlier scan, or to check that treatment is working. Your care team can tell you the specific reason yours was ordered.

entries.question: What should I bring to my neurology appointment?
entries.canonical_answer: It helps to bring a current list of your medications and doses, any symptom notes or a diary, your insurance and ID, results or records from other doctors if you have them, and a list of questions. Bringing a family member or caregiver can also help.

entries.question: How do I get my test results?
entries.canonical_answer: Test results are usually shared through your patient portal, by a call or message from the office, or at your next visit. If you haven't heard back and are waiting on results, it's reasonable to contact the clinic to ask.

entries.question: What happens after my neurology clinic visit?
entries.canonical_answer: After a visit you'll usually get a summary of the plan, which may include new or changed medications, tests to schedule, referrals, and when to follow up. If anything in your after-visit summary is unclear, the clinic can clarify it.

entries.question: How do I refill my prescription?
entries.canonical_answer: Refills are usually requested through your pharmacy or your patient portal, and the office renews them if appropriate. Request refills before you run out, since some medicines should not be stopped suddenly. Your clinic can tell you their preferred way to handle refills.

entries.question: Who do I call if I have questions between visits?
entries.canonical_answer: For non-urgent questions, your neurology clinic or its nurse line is usually the right contact, often through the patient portal or phone. For anything urgent or an emergency, call your provider's urgent line or 911. When in doubt, it's always okay to call and ask.

entries.question: How do I prepare for my first neurology appointment?
entries.canonical_answer: It helps to write down your main symptoms and when they started, bring your medication list and any prior test results, and note questions you want to ask. Arriving a little early to handle paperwork can make the visit smoother.

entries.question: What's the difference between a neurologist and a neurosurgeon?
entries.canonical_answer: A neurologist is a doctor who diagnoses and treats conditions of the brain, spine, and nerves, usually without surgery, often using medicines and other therapies. A neurosurgeon is a doctor who performs surgery on the brain, spine, and nerves. Many patients see both as part of their care.

entries.question: Is it normal to feel tired or confused after a seizure?
entries.canonical_answer: Yes. After a seizure many people have a recovery period, sometimes called the postictal phase, with tiredness, confusion, soreness, or not remembering the event. This can last minutes and sometimes longer. If recovery seems unusually long or different from your past seizures, tell your care team.

entries.question: When is a seizure an emergency?
entries.canonical_answer: Call 911 if a seizure lasts more than about 5 minutes, if another seizure starts soon after one ends, if the person does not wake up or return to normal afterward, if it's their first-ever seizure, if it happens in water, or if they're injured, pregnant, or have trouble breathing. When in doubt, call 911.

entries.question: What should I do if someone is having a seizure?
entries.canonical_answer: Help them to the ground in a safe spot, move away hard or sharp objects, cushion the head, loosen anything tight around the neck, and gently turn them on their side. Do not hold them down or put anything in their mouth, and stay with them until they recover. Call 911 if it lasts over 5 minutes or they don't wake up.

entries.question: What should I do if I miss a dose of my seizure medicine?
entries.canonical_answer: Missing doses of seizure medicine can raise the risk of a seizure, so it's important to take it consistently. What to do for a specific missed dose depends on the medicine and timing, so check the instructions that came with it or call your pharmacist or prescriber. Don't double up unless they tell you to.

entries.question: Can I drink alcohol with my seizure medication?
entries.canonical_answer: In general, alcohol can interact with seizure medicines and can itself trigger seizures, so many people are advised to limit or avoid it. Because this depends on your specific medicine and situation, confirm with the doctor who prescribed it.

entries.question: When can I drive if I have seizures?
entries.canonical_answer: Driving rules after a seizure vary by location and usually require a seizure-free period and sometimes a doctor's sign-off. Because the specifics depend on where you live and your situation, check with your provider and your local driving authority before driving.

entries.question: What can trigger a seizure?
entries.canonical_answer: Common triggers for people with epilepsy include missed medication, lack of sleep, high stress, alcohol, and sometimes flashing lights or illness. Triggers vary from person to person, so it can help to track yours and share them with your care team.

entries.question: Will I have to take seizure medication forever?
entries.canonical_answer: It depends on the cause of your seizures and how well they're controlled. Some people take medication long term, while others may eventually reduce or stop under a doctor's guidance. This is a decision to make with your neurologist, and you should not stop on your own.

entries.question: What should my family know about my seizures?
entries.canonical_answer: It helps for family to know basic seizure first aid: keep you safe, cushion your head, turn you on your side, time the seizure, and not put anything in your mouth. They should also know to call 911 if a seizure lasts over 5 minutes or you don't wake up afterward.

entries.question: What does levetiracetam (Keppra) do?
entries.canonical_answer: Levetiracetam, sold as Keppra, is a medicine used to help prevent seizures. It acts on electrical activity in the brain to make seizures less likely, and it's taken on a regular schedule to keep a steady level in your body.

entries.question: What does lamotrigine (Lamictal) do?
entries.canonical_answer: Lamotrigine, sold as Lamictal, is used to help prevent seizures and, in some people, to help stabilize mood. It's usually started at a low dose and increased slowly. If you ever develop a rash while taking it, contact your prescriber promptly.

entries.question: What does topiramate (Topamax) do?
entries.canonical_answer: Topiramate, sold as Topamax, is used to help prevent seizures and to help prevent migraines in some people. It's taken on a schedule, and your prescriber can explain what to watch for while taking it.

entries.question: What does gabapentin do?
entries.canonical_answer: Gabapentin is used for certain types of nerve pain and for some seizure conditions. It works by calming overactive nerve signaling. It's taken on a schedule, and your prescriber can tell you what to expect.

entries.question: Why am I taking aspirin after my stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: Low-dose aspirin is often used after certain strokes as an antiplatelet, meaning it helps keep blood platelets from clumping into clots, which can lower the risk of another stroke. Because aspirin isn't right for everyone, take it exactly as your prescriber directed.

entries.question: What does clopidogrel (Plavix) do?
entries.canonical_answer: Clopidogrel, sold as Plavix, is an antiplatelet medicine that helps prevent blood clots, and it's often used to lower the risk of another stroke or related events. Take it as prescribed and tell your providers you're on it, since it affects bleeding.

entries.question: Why am I on a statin like atorvastatin after my stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: Statins such as atorvastatin lower cholesterol and help stabilize blood vessels, which can reduce the risk of another stroke or heart event. They're taken long term in most cases; your prescriber can explain your specific reason and any monitoring.

entries.question: How do I know if my medication is causing side effects?
entries.canonical_answer: Many medicines have common, usually mild side effects listed in the information that comes with them. If you notice new or bothersome symptoms after starting a medicine, it's worth checking that leaflet and contacting your prescriber or pharmacist. Severe reactions, like trouble breathing or a spreading rash, need emergency care.

entries.question: Can I stop my medication once I feel better?
entries.canonical_answer: In general, you should not stop a neurology medication on your own, even if you feel better, because some medicines need to be continued or tapered slowly and stopping suddenly can cause problems. Talk with the doctor who prescribed it before making any change.

entries.question: Can I take over-the-counter medicines with my neurology medications?
entries.canonical_answer: Some over-the-counter medicines, supplements, and herbal products can interact with neurology medications. Before starting a new one, it's a good idea to check with your pharmacist or prescriber, who can look for interactions.

entries.question: When can I drive after a craniotomy?
entries.canonical_answer: Driving after brain surgery depends on your recovery, whether you've had seizures, your vision and reaction time, and sometimes local rules, so there isn't a single timeline. Your neurosurgeon will tell you when it's safe for you, and it's best not to drive until they clear you.

entries.question: When do my stitches or staples come out after brain surgery?
entries.canonical_answer: Stitches or staples after a craniotomy are usually removed at a follow-up visit once the incision has healed enough, and the exact timing is set by your surgeon. Your discharge instructions should give your specific date, so check there or call the surgeon's office.

entries.question: Are there lifting restrictions after brain surgery?
entries.canonical_answer: People are usually asked to avoid heavy lifting and straining for a period after a craniotomy, because it can raise pressure in the head. The specific weight limit and how long it lasts come from your surgeon, so follow your discharge instructions.

entries.question: When can I shower or wash my hair after brain surgery?
entries.canonical_answer: Many people can shower a few days after surgery but are asked to keep the incision care simple and gentle for a couple of weeks, sometimes avoiding certain hair products at first. Because instructions vary by surgeon and incision, follow the discharge guidance you were given.

entries.question: How do I care for my incision after brain surgery?
entries.canonical_answer: General incision care usually means keeping it clean and dry, following your surgeon's instructions on dressings, and watching for signs of infection like increasing redness, swelling, warmth, drainage, or fever. If you see those signs, contact your surgeon's office; for severe symptoms, seek urgent care.

entries.question: Is it normal to feel very tired after brain surgery?
entries.canonical_answer: Fatigue is common and can last weeks after a craniotomy as your brain heals, and resting and gradually increasing activity usually helps. If tiredness suddenly worsens or comes with new symptoms like worsening headache, confusion, or fever, contact your care team.

entries.question: When can I return to work after a craniotomy?
entries.canonical_answer: Return-to-work timing after brain surgery varies a lot depending on the surgery, your recovery, and your job. Some people return in a few weeks and others need longer. Your neurosurgeon can give you guidance based on your situation.

entries.question: Is some headache normal after brain surgery?
entries.canonical_answer: Mild headache can be expected for a while after a craniotomy as you heal. However, a severe, rapidly worsening, or new type of headache, especially with fever, vomiting, confusion, vision changes, or weakness, is not something to wait on, contact your surgeon or seek urgent care.

entries.question: When can I fly after neurosurgery?
entries.canonical_answer: Air travel after brain surgery is usually delayed for a period and depends on your specific surgery and recovery. Because there isn't a single rule, ask your neurosurgeon when flying is safe for you before booking travel.

entries.question: Why did they order both a CT and an MRI?
entries.canonical_answer: CT and MRI show different things. A CT scan is fast and good at detecting bleeding, while an MRI gives more detailed pictures of brain tissue and can show changes a CT may miss. Ordering both is common and gives the team a fuller picture.

entries.question: Is a headache normal a few days after a lumbar puncture?
entries.canonical_answer: A headache after a lumbar puncture is a known and usually self-limited effect, often worse when sitting or standing and better when lying down. Resting and staying hydrated can help. If it's severe, lasts many days, or comes with fever, a stiff neck, or new symptoms, contact your care team.

entries.question: What is a lumbar puncture or spinal tap?
entries.canonical_answer: A lumbar puncture, or spinal tap, is a test where a thin needle is used to collect a small sample of the fluid around the spinal cord, usually from the lower back. It helps diagnose certain infections, bleeding, and other neurological conditions. Your team can explain why yours was done.

entries.question: What is an EEG?
entries.canonical_answer: An EEG, or electroencephalogram, is a painless test that records the brain's electrical activity using small sensors placed on the scalp. It's often used to evaluate seizures and some other conditions. Your team can tell you how to prepare for yours.

entries.question: What is an EMG or nerve conduction study?
entries.canonical_answer: An EMG and nerve conduction study are tests that check how well your nerves and muscles are working, often used to evaluate numbness, weakness, or nerve pain. They involve small electrical pulses and sometimes a thin needle, and any discomfort is usually brief.

entries.question: What is an MRI with contrast?
entries.canonical_answer: An MRI with contrast uses a special dye, usually given through a vein, to make certain tissues or problems show up more clearly on the scan. It's commonly used and your team chooses it when the extra detail is helpful. Tell the team about kidney problems or past reactions to contrast.

entries.question: How do I prepare for an MRI?
entries.canonical_answer: For most brain MRIs you can eat normally beforehand unless told otherwise, but you'll need to remove metal objects and tell the staff about any implants, devices, or metal in your body. If you get claustrophobic, let your team know in advance, since there are ways to help.

entries.question: What is a migraine?
entries.canonical_answer: A migraine is a type of headache that is often moderate to severe and can come with nausea or sensitivity to light and sound, and sometimes warning symptoms called an aura. It's a common neurological condition with many treatment options, which your care team can discuss with you.

entries.question: When should I worry about a headache?
entries.canonical_answer: Most headaches aren't dangerous, but some warning signs deserve urgent attention: a sudden severe headache or the worst headache of your life, a headache with weakness, numbness, trouble speaking, vision loss, confusion, fever with a stiff neck, or one after a head injury. For those, call 911 or seek emergency care.

entries.question: What is neuropathy?
entries.canonical_answer: Neuropathy means damage or dysfunction of the nerves, often the ones in the hands and feet, which can cause numbness, tingling, burning, or weakness. It has many causes, and your care team can help find the cause and discuss ways to manage it.

entries.question: When can I drive again?
entries.canonical_answer: Whether and when you can drive depends on your specific condition, such as recent seizures, surgery, stroke, vision changes, or certain medications, and sometimes on local rules. Because it's individual, your care team is the right source for when driving is safe for you.
